1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[ja:ňĄžňÇşń║öšÖżŔÂ│]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Iotari]] == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (ŃÇîń╣×ÚúčŃÇŹŃüžŃü»Ńü¬ŃüĆŃÇÄňĆĄń║őŔĘśŃÇĆŃüžŃüÖŃâĘ´╝ü´╝ü) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} ňťőňşŞÚÖóňĄžňşŞŃâçŃéŞŃé┐ŃâźŃâčŃâąŃâ╝ŃéŞŃéóŃâá|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|ňůâń╝Őňőó}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|ŠŞčňÉŹňčÄňůąňžźňĹŻ}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150ÔÇô164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became [[Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan]] and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|rank]] or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. ŃÇÄ[[šÂÜŠŚąŠťČš┤Ç]]ŃÇĆ[[šą×ŔşĚŠÖ»ÚŤ▓]]3ň╣┤10Šťł29ŠŚąŠŁí == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|ň░żň╝ÁŠ░Ć|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} ňťőňşŞÚÖóňĄžňşŞŃâçŃéŞŃé┐ŃâźŃâčŃâąŃâ╝ŃéŞŃéóŃâá|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|ňůâń╝Őňőó}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|ŠŞčňÉŹňčÄňůąňžźňĹŻ}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150ÔÇô164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became [[Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan]] and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|rank]] or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. He served under {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}}, [[Empress Gemmei]], [[Empress Gensh┼Ź]], and {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} == Origin == The [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] was a clan of {{ill|Kunitsukami|en}}. [[ňĄ¬šö░ń║«]]ŃÇÄňžôŠ░Ćň«Âš│╗ňĄžŔż×ňůŞŃÇĆ[[ŔžĺňĚŁŠŤŞň║Ś]]ŃÇü1963ň╣┤ The clan's ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|fr}}, who was a distinguished figure during {{ill|Jimmu%27s_Eastern_Expedition|en}} . ŃÇÄ[[ŠŚąŠťČŠŤŞš┤Ç]]ŃÇĆšą×ŠşŽŠ«ÁŃÇéŃÇÄ[[Šľ░Šĺ░ňžôŠ░ĆÚî▓]]ŃÇĆňĄžňĺîšą×ňłąŃüžŃü»ŃÇîšą×ščąŠ┤ąňŻŽňĹŻŃÇŹŃüĘŃüÖŃéőŃÇé {{Short description|Japanese noble family}}{{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[┼îyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[┼îmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199ÔÇô217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[ja:ňĄžňÇşń║öšÖżŔÂ│]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Iotari]] == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (ŃÇîń╣×ÚúčŃÇŹŃüžŃü»Ńü¬ŃüĆŃÇÄňĆĄń║őŔĘśŃÇĆŃüžŃüÖŃâĘ´╝ü´╝ü) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} ňťőňşŞÚÖóňĄžňşŞŃâçŃéŞŃé┐ŃâźŃâčŃâąŃâ╝ŃéŞŃéóŃâá|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|ňůâń╝Őňőó}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|ŠŞčňÉŹňčÄňůąňžźňĹŻ}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150ÔÇô164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became [[Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan]] and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|rank]] or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. ŃÇÄ[[šÂÜŠŚąŠťČš┤Ç]]ŃÇĆ[[šą×ŔşĚŠÖ»ÚŤ▓]]3ň╣┤10Šťł29ŠŚąŠŁí == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|ň░żň╝ÁŠ░Ć|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. He served under {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}}, [[Empress Gemmei]], [[Empress Gensh┼Ź]], and {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} == Origin == The [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] was a clan of {{ill|Kunitsukami|en}}. [[ňĄ¬šö░ń║«]]ŃÇÄňžôŠ░Ćň«Âš│╗ňĄžŔż×ňůŞŃÇĆ[[ŔžĺňĚŁŠŤŞň║Ś]]ŃÇü1963ň╣┤ The clan's ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|fr}}, who was a distinguished figure during {{ill|Jimmu%27s_Eastern_Expedition|en}} . 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[ja:ňĄžňÇşń║öšÖżŔÂ│]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Iotari]] == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (ŃÇîń╣×ÚúčŃÇŹŃüžŃü»Ńü¬ŃüĆŃÇÄňĆĄń║őŔĘśŃÇĆŃüžŃüÖŃâĘ´╝ü´╝ü) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} ňťőňşŞÚÖóňĄžňşŞŃâçŃéŞŃé┐ŃâźŃâčŃâąŃâ╝ŃéŞŃéóŃâá|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|ňůâń╝Őňőó}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|ŠŞčňÉŹňčÄňůąňžźňĹŻ}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150ÔÇô164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became [[Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan]] and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|rank]] or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. ŃÇÄ[[šÂÜŠŚąŠťČš┤Ç]]ŃÇĆ[[šą×ŔşĚŠÖ»ÚŤ▓]]3ň╣┤10Šťł29ŠŚąŠŁí == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. (ŃÇîń╣×ÚúčŃÇŹŃüžŃü»Ńü¬ŃüĆŃÇÄňĆĄń║őŔĘśŃÇĆŃüžŃüÖŃâĘ´╝ü´╝ü) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} ňťőňşŞÚÖóňĄžňşŞŃâçŃéŞŃé┐ŃâźŃâčŃâąŃâ╝ŃéŞŃéóŃâá|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|ňůâń╝Őňőó}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|ŠŞčňÉŹňčÄňůąňžźňĹŻ}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150ÔÇô164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became [[Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan]] and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|rank]] or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en}}'s bestowed Rengo surname)}}'''Yamato no Iotari''' was [[Nobility|an aristocrat]] from [[Asuka period|the Asuka period]] to [[Nara period|the Nara period]] . His {{ill|10=ja|11=ŃéźŃâÉŃâŹ|Kabane|en}} was {{ill|10=ja|11=ň┐îň»Ş|Ikisui}} . His {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|lt=official rank|ja|ň«śńŻŹšŤŞňŻôňłÂ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kan'i s┼Źt┼Ź system|en:draft|8=en}} was Junior Fifth Rank and his job was {{ill|10=ja|11=f|Ministry_of_Justice_(pre-modern_Japan)|en|lt=Junior Assistant Minister of Justice}} . He served under {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}}, [[Empress Gemmei]], [[Empress Gensh┼Ź]], and {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} == Origin == The [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] was a clan of {{ill|Kunitsukami|en}}. [[ňĄ¬šö░ń║«]]ŃÇÄňžôŠ░Ćň«Âš│╗ňĄžŔż×ňůŞŃÇĆ[[ŔžĺňĚŁŠŤŞň║Ś]]ŃÇü1963ň╣┤ The clan's ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|fr}}, who was a distinguished figure during {{ill|Jimmu%27s_Eastern_Expedition|en}} . ŃÇÄ[[ŠŚąŠťČŠŤŞš┤Ç]]ŃÇĆšą×ŠşŽŠ«ÁŃÇéŃÇÄ[[Šľ░Šĺ░ňžôŠ░ĆÚî▓]]ŃÇĆňĄžňĺîšą×ňłąŃüžŃü»ŃÇîšą×ščąŠ┤ąňŻŽňĹŻŃÇŹŃüĘŃüÖŃéőŃÇé {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[┼îyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[┼îmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[ja:ňĄžňÇşń║öšÖżŔÂ│]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Iotari]] [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (ŃÇîń╣×ÚúčŃÇŹŃüžŃü»Ńü¬ŃüĆŃÇÄňĆĄń║őŔĘśŃÇĆŃüžŃüÖŃâĘ´╝ü´╝ü) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} ňťőňşŞÚÖóňĄžňşŞŃâçŃéŞŃé┐ŃâźŃâčŃâąŃâ╝ŃéŞŃéóŃâá|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|ňůâń╝Őňőó}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|ŠŞčňÉŹňčÄňůąňžźňĹŻ}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150ÔÇô164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became [[Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan]] and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|rank]] or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. ŃÇÄ[[šÂÜŠŚąŠťČš┤Ç]]ŃÇĆ[[šą×ŔşĚŠÖ»ÚŤ▓]]3ň╣┤10Šťł29ŠŚąŠŁí == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|ň░żň╝ÁŠ░Ć|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. His {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|lt=official rank|ja|ň«śńŻŹšŤŞňŻôňłÂ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kan'i s┼Źt┼Ź system|en:draft|8=en}} was Junior Fifth Rank and his job was {{ill|10=ja|11=f|Ministry_of_Justice_(pre-modern_Japan)|en|lt=Junior Assistant Minister of Justice}} . He served under {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}}, [[Empress Gemmei]], [[Empress Gensh┼Ź]], and {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} == Origin == The [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] was a clan of {{ill|Kunitsukami|en}}. [[ňĄ¬šö░ń║«]]ŃÇÄňžôŠ░Ćň«Âš│╗ňĄžŔż×ňůŞŃÇĆ[[ŔžĺňĚŁŠŤŞň║Ś]]ŃÇü1963ň╣┤ The clan's ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|fr}}, who was a distinguished figure during {{ill|Jimmu%27s_Eastern_Expedition|en}} . (ŃÇîń╣×ÚúčŃÇŹŃüžŃü»Ńü¬ŃüĆŃÇÄňĆĄń║őŔĘśŃÇĆŃüžŃüÖŃâĘ´╝ü´╝ü) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} ňťőňşŞÚÖóňĄžňşŞŃâçŃéŞŃé┐ŃâźŃâčŃâąŃâ╝ŃéŞŃéóŃâá|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|ňůâń╝Őňőó}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|ŠŞčňÉŹňčÄňůąňžźňĹŻ}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150ÔÇô164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became [[Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan]] and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|rank]] or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|ŠŞčňÉŹňčÄňůąňžźňĹŻ}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150ÔÇô164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became {{ill|10=ja|11=Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan}} and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth [[List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|rank]] or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|ňůâń╝Őňőó}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|ŠŞčňÉŹňčÄňůąňžźňĹŻ}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150ÔÇô164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en}}'s bestowed Rengo surname)}}{{draft}} '''Yamato no Iotari''' was [[Nobility|an aristocrat]] from [[Asuka period|the Asuka period]] to [[Nara period|the Nara period]] . His {{ill|10=ja|11=ŃéźŃâÉŃâŹ|Kabane|en}} was {{ill|10=ja|11=ň┐îň»Ş|Ikisui}} . His {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|lt=official rank|ja|ň«śńŻŹšŤŞňŻôňłÂ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kan'i s┼Źt┼Ź system|en:draft|8=en}} was Junior Fifth Rank and his job was {{ill|10=ja|11=f|Ministry_of_Justice_(pre-modern_Japan)|en|lt=Junior Assistant Minister of Justice}} . He served under {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}}, [[Empress Gemmei]], [[Empress Gensh┼Ź]], and {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} == Origin == The [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] was a clan of {{ill|Kunitsukami|en}}. [[ňĄ¬šö░ń║«]]ŃÇÄňžôŠ░Ćň«Âš│╗ňĄžŔż×ňůŞŃÇĆ[[ŔžĺňĚŁŠŤŞň║Ś]]ŃÇü1963ň╣┤ The clan's ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|fr}}, who was a distinguished figure during {{ill|Jimmu%27s_Eastern_Expedition|en}} . ŃÇÄ[[ŠŚąŠťČŠŤŞš┤Ç]]ŃÇĆšą×ŠşŽŠ«ÁŃÇéŃÇÄ[[Šľ░Šĺ░ňžôŠ░ĆÚî▓]]ŃÇĆňĄžňĺîšą×ňłąŃüžŃü»ŃÇîšą×ščąŠ┤ąňŻŽňĹŻŃÇŹŃüĘŃüÖŃéőŃÇé {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[┼îyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[┼îmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199ÔÇô217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[ja:ňĄžňÇşń║öšÖżŔÂ│]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Iotari]] [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (ŃÇîń╣×ÚúčŃÇŹŃüžŃü»Ńü¬ŃüĆŃÇÄňĆĄń║őŔĘśŃÇĆŃüžŃüÖŃâĘ´╝ü´╝ü) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} ňťőňşŞÚÖóňĄžňşŞŃâçŃéŞŃé┐ŃâźŃâčŃâąŃâ╝ŃéŞŃéóŃâá|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|ňůâń╝Őňőó}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|ŠŞčňÉŹňčÄňůąňžźňĹŻ}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150ÔÇô164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became {{ill|10=ja|11=Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan}} and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|lt=rank}} or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. His {{ill|10=ja|11=ŃéźŃâÉŃâŹ|Kabane|en}} was {{ill|10=ja|11=ň┐îň»Ş|Ikisui}} . His {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|lt=official rank|ja|ň«śńŻŹšŤŞňŻôňłÂ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kan'i s┼Źt┼Ź system|en:draft|8=en}} was Junior Fifth Rank and his job was {{ill|10=ja|11=f|Ministry_of_Justice_(pre-modern_Japan)|en|lt=Junior Assistant Minister of Justice}} . He served under {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}}, [[Empress Gemmei]], [[Empress Gensh┼Ź]], and {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} == Origin == The [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] was a clan of {{ill|Kunitsukami|en}}. [[ňĄ¬šö░ń║«]]ŃÇÄňžôŠ░Ćň«Âš│╗ňĄžŔż×ňůŞŃÇĆ[[ŔžĺňĚŁŠŤŞň║Ś]]ŃÇü1963ň╣┤ The clan's ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|fr}}, who was a distinguished figure during {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en}} . 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[ja:ňĄžňÇşń║öšÖżŔÂ│]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Iotari]] [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, (ŃÇîń╣×ÚúčŃÇŹŃüžŃü»Ńü¬ŃüĆŃÇÄňĆĄń║őŔĘśŃÇĆŃüžŃüÖŃâĘ´╝ü´╝ü) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} ňťőňşŞÚÖóňĄžňşŞŃâçŃéŞŃé┐ŃâźŃâčŃâąŃâ╝ŃéŞŃéóŃâá|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to Hibara Shrine and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|ňůâń╝Őňőó}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[┼îyamato Shrine|Oyamato Shrine]], near Hibara Shrine and [[┼îmiwa Shrine|Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|ŠŞčňÉŹňčÄňůąňžźňĹŻ}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150ÔÇô164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became {{ill|10=ja|11=Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan}} and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|lt=rank}} or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. ŃÇÄ[[šÂÜŠŚąŠťČš┤Ç]]ŃÇĆ[[šą×ŔşĚŠÖ»ÚŤ▓]]3ň╣┤10Šťł29ŠŚąŠŁí == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|ň░żň╝ÁŠ░Ć|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari]] == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became {{ill|10=ja|11=Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan}} and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|lt=rank}} or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en}}'s bestowed Rengo surname)}}{{draft}} '''Yamato no Iotari''' was [[Nobility|an aristocrat]] from [[Asuka period|the Asuka period]] to [[Nara period|the Nara period]] . His {{ill|10=ja|11=ŃéźŃâÉŃâŹ|Kabane|en}} was {{ill|10=ja|11=ň┐îň»Ş|Ikisui}} . His {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|lt=official rank|ja|ň«śńŻŹšŤŞňŻôňłÂ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kan'i s┼Źt┼Ź system|en:draft|8=en}} was Junior Fifth Rank and his job was {{ill|10=ja|11=f|Ministry_of_Justice_(pre-modern_Japan)|en|lt=Junior Assistant Minister of Justice}} . He served under {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}}, [[Empress Gemmei]], [[Empress Gensh┼Ź]], and {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} == Origin == The [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] was a clan of {{ill|Kunitsukami|en}}. [[ňĄ¬šö░ń║«]]ŃÇÄňžôŠ░Ćň«Âš│╗ňĄžŔż×ňůŞŃÇĆ[[ŔžĺňĚŁŠŤŞň║Ś]]ŃÇü1963ň╣┤ The clan's ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|fr}}, who was a distinguished figure during {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en}} . ŃÇÄ[[ŠŚąŠťČŠŤŞš┤Ç]]ŃÇĆšą×ŠşŽŠ«ÁŃÇéŃÇÄ[[Šľ░Šĺ░ňžôŠ░ĆÚî▓]]ŃÇĆňĄžňĺîšą×ňłąŃüžŃü»ŃÇîšą×ščąŠ┤ąňŻŽňĹŻŃÇŹŃüĘŃüÖŃéőŃÇé {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[┼îyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[┼îmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199ÔÇô217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[ja:ňĄžňÇşń║öšÖżŔÂ│]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Iotari]] [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (ŃÇîń╣×ÚúčŃÇŹŃüžŃü»Ńü¬ŃüĆŃÇÄňĆĄń║őŔĘśŃÇĆŃüžŃüÖŃâĘ´╝ü´╝ü) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} ňťőňşŞÚÖóňĄžňşŞŃâçŃéŞŃé┐ŃâźŃâčŃâąŃâ╝ŃéŞŃéóŃâá|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari]] == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became {{ill|10=ja|11=Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan}} and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|lt=rank}} or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. ŃÇÄ[[šÂÜŠŚąŠťČš┤Ç]]ŃÇĆ[[šą×ŔşĚŠÖ»ÚŤ▓]]3ň╣┤10Šťł29ŠŚąŠŁí == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|ň░żň╝ÁŠ░Ć|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See also == * '''{{ill|Yamato no Tehiko|ja|ňÇşŠëőňŻŽ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|lt1=Yamato no Tehiko|lt2=ŠëőňŻŽ|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Yamato no Iotari|ja|ňĄžňÇşń║öšÖżŔÂ│|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|lt1=Yamato no Iotari|lt2=ń║öšÖżŔÂ│|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|lt1=Yamogokoro no Sukune|lt2=ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒áň«┐šąó|complete=true|en:draft}} * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|ňĄžňĺîňĄžňŤŻÚşéšą×šĄż|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en||fr|Yamatototohimomoso-hime}} == footnote == == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari]] == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became {{ill|10=ja|11=Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan}} and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|lt=rank}} or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. ({{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en}}'s bestowed Rengo surname)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{complex}} '''Yamato no Iotari''' was [[Nobility|an aristocrat]] from [[Asuka period|the Asuka period]] to [[Nara period|the Nara period]] . His {{ill|10=ja|11=ŃéźŃâÉŃâŹ|Kabane|en}} was {{ill|10=ja|11=ň┐îň»Ş|Ikisui}} . He served under {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}}, [[Empress Gemmei]], [[Empress Gensh┼Ź]], and {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}}. == Origin == The [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] was a clan of {{ill|Kunitsukami|en}}. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|ŠĄÄŠá╣Š┤ąňŻŽ}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari]] == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became {{ill|10=ja|11=Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan}} and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|lt=rank}} or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|ňŞéšú»ÚĽĚň░żňŞé}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari]] == Biography == In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became {{ill|10=ja|11=Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan}} and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|lt=rank}} or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. In the first year of the reign of {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en}} ( 697 ), he traveled overland to [[Tsukushi Province]] as an envoy to welcome {{ill|10=ja|11=Šľ░šżůńŻ┐|the Silla envoys}} (his rank at the time was Shin Daiichi). [[710|In 710]], he was promoted from [[Wad┼Ź (era)|Junior]] Sixth Rank to Junior Fifth Rank . In 714, he became {{ill|10=ja|11=Š░ĆÚĽĚŔÇů|the head of the clan}} and was in charge of religious services. In the 7th year [[Y┼Źr┼Ź|of the Yoro era]] ( 723 ), Kii, a man from {{ill|Sakyo|en}}, presented a white turtle, which was deemed an auspicious omen, and as the [[kuni no miyatsuko]] (Owa kuni no miyatsuko) of the country that had brought the turtle, he was bestowed with 10 pieces of silk, 100 pieces of [[hemp]], and 20 pieces [[Textile|of cloth]] . In the third year [[Jinki (era)|of the Jinki]] era ( 726 ), he was promoted to the rank of Junior Fifth Rank. In 727, at a banquet celebrating the birth of {{ill|Emperor Sh┼Źmu|en}} 's son, Prince {{ill|10=ja|11=ňč║šÄő|Motoi}}, anyone of fifth {{ill|List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|en|lt=rank}} or higher was given [[hemp]], and especially the eldest son of a prominent family of fifth rank or higher was given an additional ten bolts of silk. However, Gohyakutaku was specially added to this list along with {{ill|10=ja|11=Ŕ¬┐ŠĚíŠÁĚ|Chotankai}} because of his advanced age.{{Cite web|last=Yu|first=A. C.|title=Tsuki no Omi - Japanese Wiki Corpus|url=https://www.japanesewiki.com/person/TSUKI%20no%20Omi.html|access-date=2024-04-09|website=www.japanesewiki.com}} Year of death unknown. His final official rank was Junior Fifth Rank and Junior Assistant Minister of Justice. ŃÇÄ[[šÂÜŠŚąŠťČš┤Ç]]ŃÇĆ[[šą×ŔşĚŠÖ»ÚŤ▓]]3ň╣┤10Šťł29ŠŚąŠŁí == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|ň░żň╝ÁŠ░Ć|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See also == * '''{{ill|Yamato no Tehiko|ja|ňÇşŠëőňŻŽ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|lt1=Yamato no Tehiko|lt2=ŠëőňŻŽ|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Yamato no Iotari|ja|ňĄžňÇşń║öšÖżŔÂ│|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|lt1=Yamato no Iotari|lt2=ń║öšÖżŔÂ│|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|ja|ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒á|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|lt1=Yamogokoro no Sukune|lt2=ňÇşňÉżňşÉš▒áň«┐šąó|complete=true|en:draft}} * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|ňĄžňĺîňĄžňŤŻÚşéšą×šĄż|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en||fr|Yamatototohimomoso-hime}} == footnote == == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (ŠŚąŠťČŠŤŞš┤Ç)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (ň«çŠ▓╗Ŕ░Ěňşč)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=ńŞŐ}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (Šľ░ŔĘé ňĆĄń║őŔĘś)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (ŠşŽšö░šąÉňÉë)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichir┼Ź Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukansh┼Ź: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Fr├ęd├ęric|Nussbaum, Louis-Fr├ęd├ęric]] and K├Ąthe Roth. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon ┼îdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H.
